steps.title: Identify what the question is asking
steps.content:
The question asks for the conclusion of Wang and colleagues' 2016 study. The notes distinguish background facts (the mutations exist; both animals have them) from the study's actual finding: the dogs first acquired these mutations by interbreeding with Tibetan wolves around 24,000 years ago. A conclusion is a causal finding, not just a description of shared traits.
  • Choice A — 'Like Tibetan mastiffs, Tibetan wolves can withstand hypoxic conditions at high altitudes.': States a shared capability but says nothing about origin or the 2016 study's finding. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Both Tibetan mastiffs and Tibetan wolves have mutations in their EPAS1 and HBB genes, which prevent excess hemoglobin production and boost hemoglobin's oxygen-carrying ability, respectively.': Describes a shared genetic trait, not the study's conclusion. Also misattributes functions. ✗
  • Choice C — 'In addition to preventing excess hemoglobin production, a mutation in Tibetan mastiffs' HBB gene boosts hemoglobin's oxygen-carrying ability.': Describes one gene's function in isolation and misrepresents the notes. Not a study conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — 'By interbreeding with Tibetan wolves around 24,000 years ago, Tibetan mastiffs acquired the genetic mutations that enable them to withstand hypoxic conditions.': Directly states the study's causal conclusion about origin and timing. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content:
The Wang study concluded that mastiffs acquired altitude-adaptation mutations through interbreeding with wolves ~24,000 years ago. Only Choice D captures this causal, time-specific finding.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
